104: **Spain**, top of **Group H** on 7 points and the only team to concede once in seven matches this tournament, meet **Argentina**, top of **Group J** on 9 points and the tournament's highest-scoring side, in Sunday's final at MetLife Stadium (12:00 PM PT) — the two sides' first competitive meeting since Argentina's 2-1 win over Spain in 1966. **Spain** beat **France** 2-0 in Dallas, with Mikel Oyarzabal's penalty — his fifth goal of the tournament, making him Spain's all-time leading scorer at the finals — and a Pedro Porro strike doing the damage; Porro is carrying a muscle issue from that semi-final and Lamine Yamal, who missed Thursday's training session with a left-thigh knock, are both expected to start, while Yeremy Pino (shoulder) remains the only confirmed absentee. **Argentina**, who beat **England** 2-1 with Enzo Fernández's 85th-minute equaliser and Lautaro Martínez's stoppage-time winner, are nursing fatigue after going to extra time twice in the knockouts, with Leandro Paredes substituted in the 64th minute against England and Cristian Romero having already battled cramps in the quarter-final against Switzerland; a win would make them only the third nation to defend the title, while Unai Simón — who conceded just once in seven matches — faces Messi for the first time in a competitive fixture.
103: **France**, who topped **Group I** with 9 points and a +8 goal difference, face **England**, winners of **Group L** on 7 points, in the third-place match at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami (2:00 PM PT) — with Kylian Mbappé's eight goals level with Messi's tally but trailing him on assists (3 to 4), and two goals today would give Mbappé 22 career tournament goals to break Messi's all-time record of 21, while Harry Kane and Jude Bellingham — each on six goals — need two to draw level with the leaders. **France** are without William Saliba (back injury, likely surgery) and sweating on goalkeeper Brice Samba (calf), with Maxence Lacroix deputising alongside Ibrahima Konaté; **England** face their own defensive reshuffle after Reece James was forced off the Argentina semi-final with a muscle injury, Jordan Henderson is confirmed out with a broken wrist, and Declan Rice's fitness has been closely managed throughout the knockouts. **England** have lost both previous third-place matches at the finals — to Italy in 1990 and Belgium in 2018 — and have won just one of their last nine matches against **France**; **France**, by contrast, have won both their previous third-place finals (6-3 vs West Germany in 1958, 4-2 vs Belgium in 1986), and this is Didier Deschamps' 27th and final match in charge, having already set the all-time record for most wins by a manager at the finals.
